schools.summary: Gill: the famine was sore in the land of Canaan; it increased yet more and more, and this is observed for the sake of what follows, showing the reason and necessity of Jacob's sons taking a second journey into Egypt; the witnesses note that the famine was sore in the land, growing ever more grievous — the pressing want that drove Jacob's house to the second journey (the narrative resuming with the deepening of the famine: 'the famine was sore in the land'; the force of the brief verse, that the famine in Canaan 'was sore,' heavy and grievous, 'increasing yet more and more,' so that the corn brought from Egypt was soon spent and the family pressed by want again; that the Scripture marks this to show 'the reason and necessity' of the brothers' second going down to Egypt, that it was the hard hand of the famine, not any willingness, that drove them; the providence of God in the famine, that it was the very means by which his hidden purpose, the bringing of all Jacob's house to Joseph, was being worked out; that the sore famine, a present grief, was an instrument of God's distant mercy).

themes: And the famine was sore in the land
the famine increasing yet more and more in Canaan
the pressing want that drove Jacob's house to a second journey
God's providence in the famine, working out his purpose of bringing all to Joseph

schools.summary: Gill: the corn was eaten up in so numerous a family as Jacob's, having so many children, grandchildren, and servants, what nine men brought on so many asses; the witnesses note that, when the corn from Egypt was eaten, Jacob bade his sons go again and buy a little food — the renewed need and the father's command to return for more corn (the spent store forcing the second errand: 'when they had eaten up the corn which they had brought out of Egypt, their father said unto them, Go again, buy us a little food'; the cause, that 'the corn which they had brought out of Egypt,' the load of nine asses, was soon 'eaten up' in so large a household as Jacob's, with his sons, their wives, children, and servants; Jacob's word, 'Go again, buy us a little food,' that the old man, pressed by want, bids them return to Egypt for more; the touch in 'a little food,' that Jacob speaks modestly, asking but a little, perhaps hoping to avoid the harder matter of sending Benjamin, of which he was loath to think; that the famine left no choice but to go down again to the man who held the corn).

themes: when they had eaten up the corn which they had brought out of Egypt
their father said unto them, Go again, buy us a little food
Jacob's command to return to Egypt when the corn was spent
asking modestly for a little, loath to think of sending Benjamin

schools.summary: Gill: Reuben the eldest had already met a repulse, Simeon was now in Egypt, and Levi perhaps, on account of the affair of Shechem, did not stand well in his father's favour, so Judah, coming to the fore, spoke; the witnesses note Judah telling his father that the man had solemnly protested they should not see his face except their brother came — the rising of Judah to plead, reminding Jacob of the strict condition laid on them (Judah taking the lead and pressing the hard truth: 'Judah spake unto him, saying, The man did solemnly protest unto us, saying, Ye shall not see my face, except your brother be with you'; the rise of Judah here, that Reuben, who had offered before, had failed to move Jacob, and now 'Judah' comes forward, the leader among the brothers, the royal and Messianic line; the substance of his word, that 'the man,' Joseph unknown to them, 'did solemnly protest,' charging them straitly, 'Ye shall not see my face, except your brother be with you,' that they could not so much as come before him, much less buy corn, unless Benjamin came down; that Judah lays the plain necessity before his father, that there was no going to Egypt without Benjamin).

themes: Judah spake unto him, saying, The man did solemnly protest unto us
Ye shall not see my face, except your brother be with you
Judah rising to the fore, the royal line, pressing the man's charge
laying the plain necessity before Jacob, no going without Benjamin

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Judah said, If thou wilt send our brother with us, we will go down and buy thee food; Gill notes the offer was conditioned on Benjamin's going under their care; the witnesses note Judah saying that if their father would send their brother with them, they would go down and buy food — the plain offer of obedience on the one needful condition, the sending of Benjamin (Judah stating the terms simply: 'If thou wilt send our brother with us, we will go down and buy thee food'; the directness of the offer, that the brothers were willing and ready to 'go down and buy thee food,' to do their father's bidding and provide for the house, on the one condition that he 'send our brother with us'; that Judah does not press beyond what was needful but lays the choice plainly before Jacob, Benjamin must go or there is no corn; the brothers' submission in this, that they would not act against the man's word nor go down in vain but waited on their father's consent to send Benjamin).

themes: If thou wilt send our brother with us
we will go down and buy thee food
Judah's plain offer to go on the one condition Benjamin be sent
the brothers willing to obey, but not to go down in vain

schools.summary: Gill: they said this not from a spirit of rebellion or disobedience to their father, nor of stubbornness and obstinacy, but because they durst not go down without Benjamin, the man having so charged them; the witnesses note Judah saying that if Benjamin were not sent, they would not go down, for the man had said they should not see his face without their brother — the firm but dutiful refusal, grounded not on stubbornness but on the man's strict charge (Judah stating the other side plainly: 'if thou wilt not send him, we will not go down: for the man said unto us, Ye shall not see my face, except your brother be with you'; the firmness, 'we will not go down,' that the brothers would not undertake a vain and fruitless journey, going to Egypt only to be turned away; the careful note Gill makes, that this was not 'rebellion' or 'stubbornness' against their father but a plain necessity, they 'durst not' go down without Benjamin, the man having so solemnly charged them, and to go without him would be to no purpose; that Judah repeats the man's word, 'Ye shall not see my face, except your brother,' to press that the case admitted no other way).

themes: if thou wilt not send him, we will not go down
for the man said unto us, Ye shall not see my face, except your brother be with you
Judah's firm refusal of a vain journey without Benjamin
grounded not on stubbornness but on the man's strict charge

schools.summary: Gill: Israel said this in answer to Judah's speech, blaming them for telling the man of another brother; the witnesses note Israel asking why they had dealt so ill with him as to tell the man they had another brother — the old father's grieved reproach, laying the blame on his sons in his distress (Jacob, pressed and pained, breaking out in complaint: 'Wherefore dealt ye so ill with me, as to tell the man whether ye had yet a brother?'; the grief in the reproach, that Jacob, loath to part with Benjamin, the last son of his beloved Rachel, charges his sons with having 'dealt... ill' with him, bringing this sorrow upon him, by telling the man 'whether ye had yet a brother'; the weakness and tenderness in this, that the old man, in his fear for Benjamin, blames his sons for what they could not well have helped, his love for the boy clouding his judgment; that Jacob, not yet seeing God's hand, looks only at the present grief and seeks someone to blame).

themes: And Israel said, Wherefore dealt ye so ill with me
as to tell the man whether ye had yet a brother?
Israel's grieved reproach, blaming his sons for telling of Benjamin
the old father, loath to part with Rachel's last son, not yet seeing God's hand

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: they said, The man asked us straitly of our state and of our kindred, saying, Is your father yet alive? have ye another brother? and we told him according to the tenor of these words; could we certainly know he would say, Bring your brother down?; the witnesses note the brethren answering that the man had asked them closely of their family, and that they could not have known he would demand Benjamin — the sons' reasonable defence, that they had but answered the man's searching questions (the brothers clearing themselves of their father's charge: 'The man asked us straitly of our state, and of our kindred... and we told him according to the tenor of these words: could we certainly know that he would say, Bring your brother down?'; their plea, that 'the man asked us straitly,' closely and pointedly, 'of our state, and of our kindred,' even asking 'Is your father yet alive? have ye another brother?' so that they had no choice but to answer truly 'according to the tenor of these words'; the reasonableness, 'could we certainly know that he would say, Bring your brother down?' that they could not have foreseen the man would demand Benjamin's coming, and so were not to blame; the providence behind it all, that the man's close questioning, Joseph's, to learn of his father and brother, was God's ordering, unknown to them).

themes: The man asked us straitly of our state, and of our kindred
could we certainly know that he would say, Bring your brother down?
the brothers' reasonable defence, having answered the man's searching questions truly
God's ordering behind the questioning, unknown to them

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the original is not yeled (from which our word lad comes) but naar (young man), Benjamin being now grown, not a child; the witnesses note Judah urging Israel to send the lad that they might live and not die, both they, their father, and their little ones — the earnest plea of Judah, pressing the choice of life against death for the whole house (Judah, the leader, making the moving appeal: 'Send the lad with me, and we will arise and go; that we may live, and not die, both we, and thou, and also our little ones'; the urgency, 'that we may live, and not die,' that Judah sets the matter as a plain choice between life and death, if Benjamin goes they may buy corn and the whole family live, if not all must perish in the famine; the breadth of the appeal, 'both we, and thou, and also our little ones,' that not the brothers only but Jacob himself and the youngest children of the house all hung on this decision; Clarke's note that 'the lad' was no infant but a grown man, Benjamin being now of full age; the wisdom and tenderness of Judah's plea, weighing the love of one son against the lives of all).

themes: Send the lad with me, and we will arise and go
that we may live, and not die, both we, and thou, and also our little ones
Judah's earnest plea, the choice of life against death for the whole house
the brothers, their father, and the little ones all hanging on the decision

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Judah said, I will be surety for him; of my hand shalt thou require him, if I bring him not unto thee then let me bear the blame for ever; Gill: Judah engaged for Benjamin's safe return; the witnesses note Judah offering to be surety for Benjamin, that Jacob should require him at his hand, and to bear the blame for ever if he failed — the great pledge of suretyship, Judah binding himself for his brother's safety, a foreshadowing of Christ the Surety (Judah giving the strongest possible bond: 'I will be surety for him; of my hand shalt thou require him: if I bring him not unto thee... then let me bear the blame for ever'; the fulness of the pledge, that Judah makes himself personally answerable, 'I will be surety for him,' undertaking to 'bring him... unto thee, and set him before thee,' and binding himself, 'of my hand shalt thou require him,' to answer with himself if Benjamin were lost; the solemn forfeit he accepts, 'let me bear the blame for ever,' that he would take the guilt and shame upon himself for ever if he failed; the typology the commentators draw, that here Judah, the head of the royal line, becomes 'surety' for his brother, a fit foreshadowing of Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, who became Surety for his brethren, answering for them with himself; that this pledge at last moved the father).

debates.question: What does Judah's suretyship for Benjamin foreshadow?

sides.position: Christ the Surety — Judah, head of the royal tribe and ancestor of Christ (the Lion of Judah), binding himself to answer with himself for his brother foreshadows the Saviour who became Surety for his brethren

themes: I will be surety for him; of my hand shalt thou require him
if I bring him not unto thee, then let me bear the blame for ever
Judah's great pledge of suretyship for Benjamin
a foreshadowing of Christ, the Lion of Judah, Surety for his brethren

schools.summary: Gill: except they had lingered and delayed going down to Egypt, through the demur Jacob made of sending Benjamin, they might have gone and come back a second time; the witnesses note Judah saying that, except for their lingering, they might already have returned a second time — the gentle reproach of the delay, that Jacob's hesitation had cost them precious time (Judah closing his plea with a word on the lost time: 'except we had lingered, surely now we had returned this second time'; the force of it, that, had they not 'lingered,' delayed through Jacob's long demur over Benjamin, they might by now have gone down to Egypt and 'returned this second time,' the journey twice done and the want twice relieved; the gentle reproach in Judah's word, that the father's hesitation, born of his love for Benjamin, had cost the family precious time while the famine pressed; that Judah urges no further delay, the matter being now plain and the need urgent; the wisdom of acting in season, that needless delay in a plain duty does harm).

themes: except we had lingered
surely now we had returned this second time
Judah's gentle reproach of the delay over Benjamin
urging that no more precious time be lost while the famine pressed

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob was in some measure convinced by their reasonings and in part reconciled to let Benjamin go, there being nothing else to be done; he bids them take of the best fruits, balm, honey, spices, myrrh, nuts, and almonds, the choice products of Canaan; the witnesses note Israel yielding, bidding them take of the best fruits of the land as a present for the man — the father's reluctant consent, sealed by a wise and conciliating gift (Jacob, overcome at last, giving way and counselling prudence: 'If it must be so now, do this; take of the best fruits in the land... and carry down the man a present, a little balm, and a little honey, spices, and myrrh, nuts, and almonds'; the resignation in 'If it must be so now,' that Jacob, convinced by their reasoning and seeing no other way, yields to the necessity of sending Benjamin; his prudence, that he bids them 'take of the best fruits in the land' as 'a present' for the man, the choice products of Canaan, to win the governor's favour; the wisdom of a conciliating gift, that even in a hard famine Jacob would not go empty-handed to a great man but sent the best the land afforded).

themes: If it must be so now, do this; take of the best fruits in the land
carry down the man a present, a little balm, and a little honey, spices, and myrrh, nuts, and almonds
Jacob's reluctant consent, sealed by a wise conciliating gift
the best fruits of Canaan to win the governor's favour

schools.summary: Gill: they were to take double money, either to buy twice as much or rather because of the greater scarcity and the price of corn now higher; and to carry back the money found in their sacks, supposing it an oversight; the witnesses note Jacob bidding them take double money and carry back the money found in their sacks' mouths, supposing it an oversight — the careful, honest counsel of the father, restoring what was not theirs (Jacob adding a wise and upright charge: 'take double money in your hand; and the money that was brought again in the mouth of your sacks, carry it again in your hand; peradventure it was an oversight'; the two parts of the counsel, first 'double money,' to have enough to buy more corn the price being now higher in the worsening famine and to provide against the cost, and second the honest restoring of 'the money that was brought again in the mouth of your sacks,' the silver mysteriously returned after the first journey, that they should 'carry it again,' not keep what was not theirs; Jacob's charitable reading of the matter, 'peradventure it was an oversight,' choosing to think it a mistake rather than a snare, and his honesty in returning it; the uprightness of the patriarch, that even in need he would not keep money wrongly come by).

themes: take double money in your hand
the money that was brought again... carry it again in your hand; peradventure it was an oversight
Jacob's careful, honest counsel: double money and restoring the silver
the uprightness of the patriarch, refusing to keep what was not his

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Take also your brother, and arise, go again unto the man; Gill: Jacob committed Benjamin into their hands and to their care, declaring his consent and willingness that he should go; the witnesses note Jacob bidding them take their brother also and go again to the man — the father's hard surrender of Benjamin, the final yielding of the beloved son (Jacob, all else being ordered, coming to the hardest word: 'Take also your brother, and arise, go again unto the man'; the weight of these few words for the old man, that to say 'Take also your brother' was to give up Benjamin, the last and dearest son of Rachel, the one he had clung to and feared to lose; that Jacob, having struggled long, now 'committed Benjamin into their hands and to their care,' declaring his consent that the boy should go; the resignation and faith mingled, that Jacob at last lets go what he most loved, trusting the matter to God; that this surrender opened the way for all the good God had prepared, the reconciliation and the saving of the house).

themes: Take also your brother
and arise, go again unto the man
Jacob's hard surrender of Benjamin, the dearest son of Rachel
resignation and faith mingled, opening the way for all God had prepared

schools.summary: Gill: God Almighty has the hearts of all men in his hands, even kings, princes, governors, the most cruel and hardhearted, and can give mercy before the man; the witnesses note Jacob praying that God Almighty give them mercy before the man, to send back both Simeon and Benjamin, and resigning himself, 'if I be bereaved, I am bereaved' — the father's committing of all to God Almighty, and his submission to whatever God should appoint (Jacob, having done all he can, casting the matter on God: 'God Almighty give you mercy before the man, that he may send away your other brother, and Benjamin'; where Jacob's hope rests, not in the present nor in his sons' wit but in 'God Almighty,' El-Shaddai, who 'has the hearts of all men in his hands' and can incline even the hard governor to 'mercy,' to send back both 'your other brother,' Simeon held in Egypt, 'and Benjamin'; the beauty of this prayer, that the old man, after all his fear and grief, turns at last to commit his sons to the almighty God; the resignation in 'If I be bereaved of my children, I am bereaved,' a word not of despair but of submission, that, having done his part, Jacob bows to whatever God should appoint, ready to accept even loss from God's hand).

debates.question: Is Jacob's 'If I be bereaved of my children, I am bereaved' despair or faith?

sides.position: The bowing of faith — not despair, but submission, that having done his part Jacob commits all to God Almighty and accepts whatever God should appoint

themes: God Almighty give you mercy before the man, that he may send away your other brother, and Benjamin
If I be bereaved of my children, I am bereaved
Jacob committing all to God Almighty, who holds all hearts
his resignation, not despair but the bowing of faith to God's will

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the men took the present and double money in their hand, and Benjamin, and went down to Egypt and stood before Joseph; the witnesses note the men taking the present, the double money, and Benjamin, going down to Egypt, and standing before Joseph — the second journey accomplished, the brothers with Benjamin now before the governor (all being made ready, they set out and arrive: 'the men took that present, and they took double money in their hand, and Benjamin; and rose up, and went down to Egypt, and stood before Joseph'; the threefold provision they carried, 'that present,' the gift of Canaan's fruits, 'double money,' for the corn and the restoring of the found silver, and above all 'Benjamin,' the brother on whom all depended; the swift telling, that they 'rose up, and went down to Egypt, and stood before Joseph,' the long-feared journey now made and the brothers, Benjamin among them, standing before the man who held their fate, all unknowing he was their brother; the providence drawing all together, the whole company save Jacob now in Joseph's presence, the dreams moving toward fulfilment).

themes: the men took that present, and they took double money in their hand, and Benjamin
and rose up, and went down to Egypt, and stood before Joseph
the second journey made, with the present, money, and Benjamin
the whole company save Jacob now before the brother they did not know

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph knew Benjamin, though he had not seen him for twenty-two years and he must be much altered, being but about ten years old when Joseph was sold; the witnesses note Joseph, seeing Benjamin, ordering his steward to bring the men home, slay, and make ready, that they should dine with him at noon — the joy of Joseph at the sight of his full brother, and his secret design of love in the feast (the sight of Benjamin moving Joseph to act: 'when Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the ruler of his house, Bring these men home, and slay, and make ready; for these men shall dine with me at noon'; the recognition, that Joseph 'knew Benjamin' at once, though twenty-two years had passed and the boy of ten was now grown, for he was his own full brother, the only other son of Rachel; Joseph's purpose, that he bids his steward 'Bring these men home' and prepare a feast, 'slay, and make ready,' that they should 'dine with me at noon,' a sudden honour that the men feared but which sprang from Joseph's love; that beneath the governor's command lay the brother's heart, longing to show kindness to his own and to draw nearer to Benjamin).

themes: when Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the ruler of his house, Bring these men home
for these men shall dine with me at noon
Joseph's joy at the sight of his full brother Benjamin
the brother's heart of love beneath the governor's command

schools.summary: Gill: the man showed them the way to it, introduced them, and led them into some apartment in it; the witnesses note the steward doing as Joseph bade and bringing the men into Joseph's house — the obedient steward carrying out his lord's command, leading the brothers in (the steward fulfilling the order: 'the man did as Joseph bade; and the man brought the men into Joseph's house'; the prompt obedience of the steward, 'the man did as Joseph bade,' carrying out his lord's command without question and bringing 'the men into Joseph's house,' leading them in and lodging them in some apartment; that this bringing into the governor's own house was an unusual and high honour, not the way travellers who came to buy corn were dealt with, which is why it filled the brothers with fear; the providence in it, that the brothers were thus drawn step by step nearer to Joseph and to the reconciliation he was preparing, though to them it seemed a thing to dread).

themes: the man did as Joseph bade
and the man brought the men into Joseph's house
the steward obediently bringing the brothers into Joseph's house
an honour they dreaded, the providence drawing them nearer to Joseph

schools.summary: Gill: it was not usual, as Jarchi observes, for those that came to buy corn to lodge there, but at an inn in the city, hence their fear; the witnesses note the men afraid at being brought into Joseph's house, fearing it was because of the returned money, that he might seize them as bondmen — the guilty, fearful suspicion of the brothers, reading the kindness as a snare (the unexpected honour filling them with dread: 'the men were afraid, because they were brought into Joseph's house; and they said, Because of the money... are we brought in; that he may seek occasion against us, and fall upon us, and take us for bondmen, and our asses'; the cause of their fear, that it was unusual for corn-buyers to be brought into the governor's house, and so they suspected some hidden purpose; their guilty reading of it, that they laid it to 'the money that was returned in our sacks,' fearing the governor meant to 'seek occasion against' them, to 'fall upon' them, and 'take us for bondmen, and our asses'; that a guilty conscience reads every kindness as a threat, the brothers, still burdened by the old sin against Joseph, imagining a snare where love was preparing a feast; the irony, that they dreaded slavery in the very house where their brother meant to honour them).

themes: the men were afraid, because they were brought into Joseph's house
that he may seek occasion against us, and fall upon us, and take us for bondmen
the brothers' guilty fear, reading the kindness as a snare
a guilty conscience reading every kindness as a threat, imagining a snare where love prepared a feast

schools.summary: Gill: the steward, the same before called the ruler of his house, under whose direction they were; they came to him just before they entered the house; the witnesses note the brethren coming near to the steward and communing with him at the door of the house — the anxious approach of the brothers, hastening to clear themselves before entering (fearful of the worst, the brothers seeking to set the matter right at once: 'they came near to the steward of Joseph's house, and they communed with him at the door of the house'; the haste and the place, that they 'came near to the steward,' the ruler of Joseph's house under whose charge they were, and 'communed with him at the door,' before they should be brought fully in, anxious to explain the matter of the money before any charge could be laid; the prudence in this, that a clear conscience seeks to clear itself, and the brothers, fearing they would be accused of theft, would speak first and openly; that their coming to the steward at the door shows their dread and their desire to be honest).

themes: they came near to the steward of Joseph's house
and they communed with him at the door of the house
the brothers anxiously approaching to clear themselves before entering
a clear conscience seeking to clear itself before any charge could be laid

schools.summary: Gill: one said in the name of the rest, perhaps Judah, 'on me let the blame lie' if guilty of rudeness in addressing thee, beginning their address with respect; the witnesses note the brethren saying, 'O sir, we came down at the first time to buy food' — the humble and respectful opening of their explanation, owning their honest errand (the brothers beginning courteously to lay the matter open: 'O sir, we came indeed down at the first time to buy food'; the respectfulness, 'O sir,' that they address the steward with all courtesy and submission, anxious to give no offence; the plain statement of their honest purpose, 'we came indeed down at the first time to buy food,' that their first journey had been only to buy corn, no other and no dishonest intent; that they begin at the beginning, meaning to explain how the money came to be in their sacks and to clear themselves of any charge of theft; the care of honest men to make their innocence plain).

themes: O sir, we came indeed down at the first time
to buy food
the brothers' respectful opening of their explanation
addressing the steward with courtesy, anxious to clear themselves

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'the inn' is the lodging-place, the place where they stopped on the journey; the witnesses note the brethren explaining that, at the lodging-place, they found every man's money in full weight in his sack, and have brought it back — the honest account of the returned silver, offering it back in full (the brothers telling plainly what they found: 'when we came to the inn... every man's money was in the mouth of his sack, our money in full weight: and we have brought it again in our hand'; the account, that at 'the inn,' the lodging-place on the road, they 'opened our sacks' and found 'every man's money... in the mouth of his sack,' and that 'in full weight,' none missing; their honesty, 'we have brought it again in our hand,' that, finding the silver returned, they did not keep it but carried it back to restore it, not willing to be enriched by what was not theirs; the care to declare the full weight, that nothing was taken or lacking, that their innocence might be plain; the uprightness of the brothers in this, proof of an honest intent).

themes: every man's money was in the mouth of his sack, our money in full weight
and we have brought it again in our hand
the honest account of the silver found and brought back
not willing to be enriched by what was not theirs, proof of an honest intent

schools.summary: Gill: they brought double money for a double quantity, or because the price was doubled, and their bringing this besides showed their honesty; the witnesses note the brethren saying they had brought other money to buy food, and could not tell who put their first money in their sacks — the full and honest declaration, owning their ignorance of the returned money's source (the brothers completing their explanation: 'other money have we brought down in our hands to buy food: we cannot tell who put our money in our sacks'; the proof of honesty in it, that, besides restoring the first money, they had 'brought down... other money' to buy more corn, so that they came not to cheat but to pay fully; their plain confession of ignorance, 'we cannot tell who put our money in our sacks,' that they had no notion how the silver came to be returned, declaring openly that it was no doing of theirs; the transparency of the brothers, hiding nothing and owning what they could not explain, the marks of honest men with nothing to conceal; that this honest dealing was about to be met with grace from the steward).

themes: other money have we brought down in our hands to buy food
we cannot tell who put our money in our sacks
the brothers' full, honest declaration of their ignorance
the transparency of honest men, hiding nothing and owning what they could not explain

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the steward's address was full of kindness, calming their fears; Gill: he bade them not be uneasy, they had nothing to fear; the witnesses note the steward saying, 'Peace be to you, fear not: your God hath given you treasure in your sacks; I had your money,' and bringing Simeon out — the steward's gracious word that stilled their fear and owned God's hand, and the restoring of Simeon (the dreaded steward speaking only kindness: 'Peace be to you, fear not: your God, and the God of your father, hath given you treasure in your sacks: I had your money. And he brought Simeon out unto them'; the comfort of the words, 'Peace be to you, fear not,' that, instead of the charge they dreaded, the steward sets their minds at rest; the remarkable confession, 'your God, and the God of your father, hath given you treasure in your sacks,' that the steward, instructed by Joseph or himself touched, ascribes the returned money to God's gift not their theft, and adds 'I had your money,' that the account was settled; the joy of 'he brought Simeon out unto them,' restoring the brother held in pledge; God's mercy, that the fear of the guilty was met with peace and a confession of God's goodness).

themes: Peace be to you, fear not: your God... hath given you treasure in your sacks
I had your money. And he brought Simeon out unto them
the steward's gracious word, ascribing the money to God's gift
the fear of the guilty met with peace and Simeon restored

schools.summary: Gill: the man brought them in after their minds were made easy, both about the money and by bringing Simeon to them; the witnesses note the steward bringing the men into the house, giving them water to wash their feet, and provender for their asses — the courteous hospitality shown the brothers, their fear now stilled (the dreaded house becoming a place of welcome: 'the man brought the men into Joseph's house, and gave them water, and they washed their feet; and he gave their asses provender'; the change from fear to welcome, that, their minds now eased about the money and Simeon restored, the brothers are brought in and treated as honoured guests, given 'water' to 'wash their feet,' the customary refreshment of travellers, and 'provender' for their 'asses'; the courtesy of the East and the kindness of Joseph's house, that the brothers, who had feared bondage, were received with the offices of hospitality; that all this was Joseph's doing, the brother's love providing for his own even before he made himself known).

themes: the man brought the men into Joseph's house, and gave them water, and they washed their feet
and he gave their asses provender
the brothers welcomed and refreshed, their fear now stilled
Joseph's love providing for his own even before he made himself known

schools.summary: Gill: they took the present out of their vessels in which they brought it, having unladen their asses, and disposed of it ready to present; the witnesses note the brethren making ready the present against Joseph's coming at noon, for they heard they should eat there — the brothers arranging their gift in expectation of the governor's coming (learning they would dine there, the brothers preparing their offering: 'they made ready the present against Joseph came at noon: for they heard that they should eat bread there'; the preparation, that the brothers 'made ready the present,' the balm, honey, spices, and the rest, taking it out and arranging it fittingly to lay before the governor when he came 'at noon'; the reason, 'for they heard that they should eat bread there,' that, being told they should dine with the man, they made ready to honour him with the gift before the meal as was the custom in approaching a great man; the providence in all this, that the brothers, all unknowing, were preparing to bow before Joseph with a present even as his dreams had foreshown).

themes: they made ready the present against Joseph came at noon
for they heard that they should eat bread there
the brothers preparing their gift to honour the governor
all unknowing preparing to bow before Joseph with a present, as the dreams foreshown

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the great respect Joseph's brethren paid to him, bringing him the present and bowing to the earth; the witnesses note the brethren, when Joseph came home, bringing him the present and bowing themselves to the earth before him — the second great fulfilment of the dream, the brothers, now with Benjamin, bowing to Joseph (the dream fulfilled again before Joseph's eyes: 'when Joseph came home, they brought him the present which was in their hand into the house, and bowed themselves to him to the earth'; the homage, that the brothers 'brought him the present' and 'bowed themselves to him to the earth,' doing the lowliest reverence before the governor; the fulfilment of the dreams the commentators mark, that here again, and now more fully, Benjamin being present, the sheaves and the stars bowed to Joseph, the brothers prostrate before the very one they had cast into the pit and sold; the providence, that what Joseph had dreamed at seventeen was now coming exactly to pass, the brothers bowing to the earth before him; the patience of God's purpose, working through long years to this hour).

debates.question: What is fulfilled in the brothers bowing to Joseph?

sides.position: Joseph's youthful dreams (Genesis 37) — the sheaves and the stars bowing — now fulfilled, the brothers prostrate before the one they had sold, God's purpose worked out through long years

themes: when Joseph came home, they brought him the present which was in their hand into the house
and bowed themselves to him to the earth
the dream fulfilled again, now more fully with Benjamin present
the brothers prostrate before the very one they had cast into the pit and sold

schools.summary: Gill: he asked them of their welfare, their peace and prosperity, especially of the health of their father, whether he was well and yet alive after so long a journey; the witnesses note Joseph asking of their welfare and of their father, the old man of whom they spake, whether he was yet alive — the brother's tender concern breaking through the governor's questions, his love for his father (Joseph, restraining himself, asking the questions dearest to his heart: 'he asked them of their welfare, and said, Is your father well, the old man of whom ye spake? Is he yet alive?'; the warmth beneath the formality, that Joseph 'asked them of their welfare,' their peace and prosperity, and then, with evident tenderness, asks after 'your father... the old man of whom ye spake,' whether 'he yet alive'; the love of Joseph for his aged father, that, twenty-two years parted, he longs to know if Jacob still lives; that the governor's courteous inquiry was in truth the yearning of a son, the brother's heart pressing toward the family he had lost; that Joseph, still hiding himself, could not hide his longing after his father).

themes: he asked them of their welfare, and said, Is your father well, the old man of whom ye spake?
Is he yet alive?
Joseph's tender inquiry after his father
the son's longing breaking through the governor's formal questions

schools.summary: Gill: by calling their father Joseph's servant, they did obeisance to him; and they bowed down their heads; the witnesses note the brethren answering that their father was in good health and yet alive, and bowing their heads in obeisance — the answer that gladdened Joseph's heart, and the renewed bowing of the brothers (the brothers giving the answer Joseph longed to hear: 'Thy servant our father is in good health, he is yet alive. And they bowed down their heads, and made obeisance'; the welcome news, 'Thy servant our father is in good health, he is yet alive,' that Jacob still lived, the word that must have gladdened Joseph's heart above all; the lowliness of their speech, calling their father 'thy servant,' and themselves 'bowed down their heads, and made obeisance' before the governor, again fulfilling the dream that the family should bow to Joseph; that the brothers, all unknowing, both told Joseph the thing he most wished to know and did him the homage his dreams had foreshown; the providence weaving the answer and the obeisance together).

themes: Thy servant our father is in good health, he is yet alive
And they bowed down their heads, and made obeisance
the answer that gladdened Joseph's heart, that his father lived
the renewed bowing, again fulfilling the dream, all unknowing

schools.summary: Gill: he had seen Benjamin before when his brethren first presented themselves, but then took no particular notice; now he fixes his eyes on him; the witnesses note Joseph, lifting his eyes and seeing Benjamin his full brother, asking if this were their younger brother and blessing him, 'God be gracious unto thee, my son' — the moving moment when Joseph greets his only full brother with a blessing of love (Joseph's heart turning to Benjamin: 'he lifted up his eyes, and saw his brother Benjamin, his mother's son, and said, Is this your younger brother... God be gracious unto thee, my son'; the tenderness, that Joseph 'saw his brother Benjamin, his mother's son,' the only other child of his beloved mother Rachel, whom he had last seen a little child; his restrained greeting, 'Is this your younger brother, of whom ye spake unto me?' asking as the governor yet his heart full; and then the gracious blessing, 'God be gracious unto thee, my son,' a word of warm affection and prayer over the young man; that Joseph, still hiding himself, could scarce contain his love at the sight of his full brother).

themes: he lifted up his eyes, and saw his brother Benjamin, his mother's son
God be gracious unto thee, my son
Joseph's tender greeting of his only full brother, the son of Rachel
restrained as the governor yet his heart full, scarce able to contain his love

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph made haste to get out of the room where he was with his brethren as fast as he could; the witnesses note Joseph, his heart yearning over Benjamin, hastening to seek where to weep, and weeping in his chamber — the overflowing of Joseph's love, that he must hide to give way to his tears (the sight of Benjamin overcoming Joseph: 'Joseph made haste; for his bowels did yearn upon his brother: and he sought where to weep; and he entered into his chamber, and wept there'; the depth of his feeling, that 'his bowels did yearn upon his brother,' that his heart was so moved with love and longing at the sight of Benjamin, his only full brother, that he could not contain himself; that he 'made haste' and 'sought where to weep,' needing to withdraw lest his tears betray him, and 'entered into his chamber, and wept there,' giving way in private to the flood of his love and grief; the tenderness of Joseph, the great governor weeping like a child for love of his brother; that this hidden weeping shows the warmth of the heart beneath the disguise, the love that would soon break forth in full).

themes: Joseph made haste; for his bowels did yearn upon his brother
and he entered into his chamber, and wept there
Joseph's heart yearning over Benjamin, withdrawing to weep
the governor weeping like a child for love, the heart beneath the disguise

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he washed his face and went out, and refrained himself, and said, Set on bread; Gill: he washed his face from the tears on it, that it might not be discerned he had been weeping; the witnesses note Joseph washing his face, going out, restraining himself, and bidding them set on bread — the mastering of his emotion, that he might keep his disguise and proceed with the feast (Joseph composing himself and returning: 'he washed his face, and went out, and refrained himself, and said, Set on bread'; the self-command of Joseph, that, having wept in private, he 'washed his face' to remove the traces of his tears, 'that it might not be discerned that he had been weeping,' and 'went out, and refrained himself,' mastering his feeling so as to keep his disguise yet a little longer; the patience of Joseph in this, that, though his heart was full and he longed to make himself known, he held back, that God's purpose might be fully accomplished in its time; that he then ordered the meal, 'Set on bread,' carrying on the feast as the governor; the strength of a self-controlled love, that waits for the right moment).

themes: he washed his face, and went out, and refrained himself
and said, Set on bread
Joseph mastering his emotion to keep his disguise and proceed
the patience of a self-controlled love, waiting for the right moment

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph ate by himself, either because he was a Hebrew or rather as a great man; the Egyptians might not eat with the Hebrews, it being an abomination to them (the Hebrews being shepherds and eating of beasts the Egyptians held sacred); the witnesses note the three tables, Joseph by himself, the brothers by themselves, and the Egyptians apart, because the Egyptians might not eat with the Hebrews — the strict Egyptian separation at the meal, the custom that kept the peoples apart (the feast set in three companies: 'they set on for him by himself, and for them by themselves, and for the Egyptians... by themselves: because the Egyptians might not eat bread with the Hebrews; for that is an abomination unto the Egyptians'; the threefold separation, Joseph eating 'by himself' as befitted his rank or as a Hebrew, the brothers 'by themselves,' and 'the Egyptians' who served 'by themselves'; the reason, 'because the Egyptians might not eat bread with the Hebrews; for that is an abomination unto the Egyptians,' that the Egyptians, by their religion and custom, counted it an 'abomination' to eat with foreigners and shepherds; the providence even in this separation, which would keep Israel a distinct people in Egypt, unmingled with the Egyptians).

themes: they set on for him by himself, and for them by themselves, and for the Egyptians by themselves
because the Egyptians might not eat bread with the Hebrews; for that is an abomination unto the Egyptians
the three separate tables, the strict Egyptian separation
a providence to keep Israel a distinct people in Egypt, unmingled

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth, and the men marvelled; the witnesses note the brethren seated before Joseph in the order of their birth, from eldest to youngest, and marvelling at it — the strange seating that astonished them (the brothers placed in a way that astonished them: 'they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth: and the men marvelled one at another'; the strange seating, that the brothers were set 'before him' in the exact order of their ages, 'the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth,' each in his right place; their amazement, 'the men marvelled one at another,' that they wondered how this Egyptian governor could know their ages and seat them so exactly, a stranger to them; the providence in it, that Joseph, knowing them all, ordered the seating to fit their ages, which to them seemed an uncanny knowledge; that this little marvel was one of many touches by which the truth was drawing near, though they did not yet perceive it).

themes: they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth
and the men marvelled one at another
the brothers seated in the exact order of their ages
wondering how the governor knew their birth-order, the truth drawing near

schools.summary: Gill: the several dishes were brought before him, who cut them up and sent to everyone their part and portion, as was usual; Benjamin's mess was five times so much as any of theirs; the witnesses note Joseph sending portions to his brethren, Benjamin's being five times as much, and the brethren drinking and being merry with him — the marked favour shown to Benjamin and the glad fellowship of the feast (Joseph honouring his full brother above the rest: 'he took and sent messes unto them from before him: but Benjamin's mess was five times so much as any of theirs. And they drank, and were merry with him'; the custom and the favour, that Joseph, as host, 'sent messes,' portions, from his own table to each, an honour, but to 'Benjamin' he sent 'five times so much,' a marked and lavish favour to his beloved full brother; the commentators' observation that Joseph showed this special love openly, perhaps also to test whether the old envy still lived in the brothers, it did not, for they were merry; the gladness of the feast, 'they drank, and were merry with him,' that the brothers, their fears now gone, ate and rejoiced with the governor, a foretaste of the full reconciliation to come).

themes: Benjamin's mess was five times so much as any of theirs
And they drank, and were merry with him
Joseph honouring Benjamin with a lavish fivefold portion
the glad fellowship of the feast, a foretaste of the full reconciliation to come
